facts.label: THE FREE-KICK REBELLION
facts.story: Late against Brazil in Gelsenkirchen, with Rivellino lining up over a free kick, Zaire defender Mwepu Ilunga bolted from the wall and hoofed the ball halfway up the pitch before anyone could touch it. Commentators wrote it off as comic naivety for decades. Ilunga later admitted the truth: he understood the rules perfectly and was begging referee Nicolae Rainea for a red card, a silent protest against a regime withholding the team's World Cup bonuses. He got only a yellow.

facts.label: GREEN, GOLD AND THE LEOPARDS
facts.story: Zaire's kit echoed the national flag dictator Mobutu Sese Seko had designed in 1971: green field, gold circle, an arm gripping a flaming torch. Nicknamed the Leopards, the team wore that green and gold to Africa Cup of Nations titles in 1968 and 1974, the second arriving three months before their World Cup debut. Mobutu rewarded the champions with cars and houses, then banned every player from leaving the country after their on-field revolt in West Germany.

1974.headline: Leopards' Historic Debut, Infamous Ending

annotations.label: A RESPECTABLE OPENER
annotations.story: Zaire's World Cup debut in Dortmund saw them hold the Scots scoreless deep into the first half before Peter Lorimer and Joe Jordan struck either side of halftime. The 0–2 scoreline flattered nobody — it stood as Zaire's most composed 90 minutes of the tournament, before financial chaos consumed the squad.

annotations.label: NINE GOALS, ONE MUTINY
annotations.story: Furious over unpaid World Cup bonuses, Zaire's players reportedly threatened to strike before facing Yugoslavia in Gelsenkirchen. Presidential pressure forced them onto the pitch demoralized, and Yugoslavia poured in nine unanswered goals — still among the heaviest defeats in World Cup history, with six scored before halftime alone.

annotations.label: ILUNGA'S CHARGE
annotations.story: Reportedly warned they'd be barred from home if they lost by four or more, Zaire trailed just 1–0 at half against Brazil. Late in the match, defender Mwepu Ilunga burst from the defensive wall and booted a Brazilian free kick upfield before it was even taken — later revealed as deliberate protest, not comic confusion.

1974.homeColors: green shirt, yellow shorts, green socks — echoing the green-and-gold Zairian flag
1974.awayColors: yellow shirt, green shorts, yellow socks
